one_line_takeaway: A grounded measurement study showing that people routinely leak identifiable and deeply sensitive information to commercial chatbots -- >70% of WildChat queries contain detectable PII and ~15% carry sensitive topics PII detectors entirely miss -- proving redaction-based defenses are insufficient and motivating context-aware nudges and local/private models.
key_finding: Even after WildChat's own Presidio-based PII scrub, over 70% of sampled queries still contain some detected PII (dominated by person and organization names), and almost 15% mention a non-PII sensitive topic that PII detectors miss entirely. PII shows up in surprising task contexts -- ~48-50% of translation queries and ~16% of code-editing queries contain detected PII. The sensitive-topic distribution over the 5k sample: academic & education 29.9%, quoted code (with live API tokens/admin IDs) 19.5%, fandom 14.0%, hobbies & habits 8.7%, financial & corporate 7.2%, sexual & erotic 6.3%, job/visa/applications 4.2%, healthcare 4.1%, personal relationships 3.3%, emotions & mental health 2.0%, politics & religion 0.7%. Storytelling/role-play/jailbreaking are frequent sites of erotic and fandom content whose sensitivity (and often-real names) evades PII tooling, demonstrating that PII detection is necessary but far from sufficient for chatbot-conversation privacy.
core_contribution: An empirical measurement study of the personal and sensitive information that real users disclose to commercial GPT chatbots. Working from the WildChat corpus of ~1M consent-collected human-GPT conversations, the authors (1) build a bottom-up taxonomy of 21 conversational task categories and an 11-category taxonomy of sensitive topics, (2) auto-annotate a 5k-conversation sample with GPT-4 (validated against human annotation), and (3) quantify both traditional PII (via Azure AI Language) and non-PII sensitive topics, showing that PII detection alone badly under-captures the sensitive content people share. They release the taxonomies and annotations. Note: leakage-via-memorization, sanitization methods, and prior self-disclosure detection are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution -- the paper's own novelty is the task/topic taxonomy plus the disclosure measurements over in-the-wild chatbot logs.
threat_model: Not a formal adversary model; the paper frames privacy risk from real user behavior. Users -- knowingly or not -- disclose PII and sensitive topics (drug use, sexual preferences, job/visa applications, financial data, mental health) to commercial LLM chatbots. That data is then (a) retained by the chatbot company and reusable for training/RLHF, ad targeting, or sale to data brokers, (b) exposed to hacks/breaches/ransomware, and (c) in WildChat's case re-published to a public research dataset despite one prior PII-removal pass. The paper stresses that rule-based/commercial PII detectors miss much of the sensitive content, so redaction-based defenses give false assurance. Recommended mitigations: local/private models, encrypted data, differential privacy, and context-aware user 'nudges' before data leaves the client.
limitations: (1) Findings are English-only and may not generalize across languages. (2) User behavior evolves, so measurements are a snapshot. (3) Selection bias: because WildChat trades free access for data sharing (and lets users avoid IP bans on OpenAI directly), it may over-represent sensitive/disallowed activity such as erotica and jailbreaking. (4) Annotation relies on GPT-4 auto-labeling validated only on 20 samples per category (mean 87-89% accuracy), and PII counts inherit Azure detector errors (e.g., code/SDK constructs mislabeled as URLs -- inflating ShareGPT's URL count -- and code as organizations). (5) In storytelling, it is often impossible to tell whether flagged names/ages/locations are real or fictional, complicating any real-vs-noise disclosure estimate. (6) The paper measures disclosure but does not evaluate any proposed nudging/mitigation mechanism.

datasets_used: WildChat (Zhao et al. 2024) -- ~1M consent-collected human-GPT-3.5/GPT-4 conversations; primary corpus, English-filtered
ShareGPT (Chiang et al. 2023) -- 1k-conversation comparison sample of user-shared GPT conversations
5k WildChat sample (one conversation per hashed IP, English, prefix/length filtered) -- main annotated set
1k unfiltered WildChat sample and 10K random WildChat conversations used for LDA topic modeling / 300 hand-annotated
quantitative_results: Over 70% of sampled WildChat queries contain some detected PII (after WildChat's own Presidio scrub)
Almost 15% of queries mention a non-PII sensitive topic
~48-50% of translation queries contain detected PII; ~16% of code-editing/debugging queries contain PII

Sensitive-topic prevalence in 5k sample: academic & education 29.9%, quoted code 19.5%, fandom 14.0%, hobbies & habits 8.7%, financial & corporate 7.2%, sexual & erotic 6.3%, job/visa/applications 4.2%, healthcare 4.1%, personal relationships 3.3%, emotions & mental health 2.0%, politics & religion 0.7%
GPT-4 task-annotation validation mean accuracy 89.2% (3 of 21 tasks dropped for <70%, leaving 18); sensitive-topic mean accuracy 87% (1 topic dropped)
Person and organization names are the most frequent detected PII entities in both WildChat and ShareGPT; email/physical-address/IP-address least frequent
baselines_compared: Traditional/rule-based PII detection (Microsoft Presidio, used by WildChat curators) -- shown to miss non-well-formatted PII
Commercial Azure AI Language PII detector (20+ categories) -- the paper's PII measurement tool, contrasted against its own sensitive-topic taxonomy which captures content PII detectors miss
Cross-corpus comparison: WildChat (sampled-per-user vs random) vs ShareGPT task and PII distributions
Prior usage study Ouyang et al. 2023 (ShareGPT-based) contrasted as lacking user consent
